array.description: The array type in Flow9 represents an immutable, ordered sequence of elements of the same type. Arrays are the primary collection type and are created using bracket syntax [1, 2, 3]. Since Flow9 arrays are persistent, operations that modify content return new arrays while leaving the original unchanged.

length.description: Returns the number of elements in the array.

concat.description: Returns a new array that is the concatenation of the two input arrays.

map.description: Returns a new array with the function applied to each element of the input array.

filter.description: Returns a new array containing only the elements for which the predicate function returns true.

fold.description: Reduces the array to a single value by applying the function to the accumulator and each element from left to right.

arrayPush.description: Returns a new array with the given value appended as the last element.

tree.description: The Tree type in Flow9 is an immutable balanced binary search tree (similar to a sorted map/dictionary) that maps keys to values. Trees provide O(log n) lookup, insertion, and deletion. They are the standard key-value data structure when ordered access or efficient lookup by key is needed.

makeTree.description: Creates a new empty tree with no key-value bindings.

setTree.description: Returns a new tree with the given key-value pair added or updated.

lookupTree.description: Looks up a key in the tree, returning Some(value) if found or None() if absent.

removeFromTree.description: Returns a new tree with the specified key and its value removed.

foldTree.description: Folds over all key-value pairs in the tree in order, accumulating a result.

string.description: The string type in Flow9 represents an immutable sequence of Unicode characters. Strings support concatenation with the + operator and various built-in functions for searching, splitting, trimming, and converting. String literals are enclosed in double quotes with standard escape sequences.

strlen.description: Returns the length of the string in characters.

strContainsAt.signature: strContainsAt(str : string, pos : int, substr : string) -> bool
strContainsAt.description: Returns true if the substring is found at the specified position in the string.

substring.signature: substring(s : string, start : int, length : int) -> string
substring.description: Returns a substring starting at the given index with the specified length.

strIndexOf.description: Returns the index of the first occurrence of the substring, or -1 if not found.

toLowerCase.description: Returns a new string with all characters converted to lowercase.

ref.description: A mutable reference cell in Flow9 that holds a value of type T and can be updated in place. Refs are the primary mechanism for mutable state in the otherwise functional Flow9 language. Created with ref(value), read with ^refVar (dereference), and updated with refVar := newValue.

maybe.description: A union type representing an optional value that may or may not be present. Maybe is used throughout Flow9 for safe handling of possibly-absent values, particularly as return types from lookups in trees and arrays. Pattern matching with switch is used to extract the contained value.

Some.description: Wraps a present value in the Maybe type, indicating the value exists.

None.description: Represents the absence of a value in the Maybe type.

either.description: Extracts the value from a Maybe, returning the default if it is None.

int.description: A 32-bit signed integer type in Flow9 for representing whole numbers. Supports standard arithmetic operations (+, -, *, /, %), comparison operators, and bitwise operations. Integer division truncates toward zero.

double.description: A 64-bit double-precision floating-point number type in Flow9 for representing decimal numbers. Follows IEEE 754 standard. Supports standard arithmetic operations and mathematical functions from the math module.

bool.description: A Boolean type in Flow9 with two possible values: true and false. Supports logical operators && (and), || (or), and ! (not). Used in conditional expressions, filter predicates, and control flow statements.
